caveats: a: The occupation source is NAICS 611500 rather than 611513 and includes cosmetology, flight, and other technical schools.
a: The estimate concerns current not-yet-automated tasks; it does not assume that all generative-AI-assisted work becomes labor substitution.
a: The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization divisor, so it has the stated vintage and basis limitations.
rho: The Census adoption rates cover all industries and changed question wording in November 2025 to include any business function.
rho: Federal guidance and portals demonstrate enablement, not measured productivity or headcount avoidance.
rho: Small LMM providers may adopt later than larger institutions because integrations and data governance have fixed costs.
e: No source measures lens eligibility among $1-10M EBITDA firms in NAICS 611513.
e: SUSB private-firm counts can include organizational forms whose economics or governance are not practically transferable.
e: The frozen firm count is a margin-bridged estimate using broader size-class data and an education-sector margin.
s5: Owner age is an economy-wide and dated proxy, not observed deal flow for this industry.
s5: A transfer may require customer, grant, credential, lease, or sponsor approvals and therefore may not qualify even when ownership changes.
s5: Deaths, closures, internal reorganizations, and transfers without operating continuity are excluded.
q: The sponsor payment survey is old and covers registered apprenticeship broadly, not LMM NAICS 611513 providers.
q: Public funding rules vary substantially by state and program, and some savings may be passed through as lower reimbursable cost.
q: The estimate excludes demand loss and implementation difficulty, which belong in other primitives.
d5: Active apprentices, total people served, programs, and outsourced training demand are different quantities.
d5: Policy goals are not forecasts and may change with appropriations or administration priorities.
d5: The estimate holds service quality and price constant and does not count new AI-training content as price inflation.
o: No observed source measures the share of apprenticeship-training quantity that will still require an external accountable operator in five years.
o: Regulatory requirements establish accountability but do not guarantee that a lens firm, rather than an employer, union, or college, supplies it.
o: Software may complement human mentors more than it replaces them, while administrative intermediation may be more substitutable.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring employer, sponsor, association, or workforce-system contracts and per-apprentice or per-cohort instruction fees, sometimes supplemented or reimbursed by federal or state workforce funds and tuition support.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive employer training units, government agencies, public colleges, labor unions and joint labor-management trusts that are not transferable control businesses, grant-only vehicles without recurring customers, and organizations whose primary activity falls outside NAICS 611513.
lens.included_activities: Privately controlled firms that repeatedly provide apprenticeship-program design, related technical instruction, curriculum, apprentice administration, compliance, and multi-employer coordination to external employers, sponsors, or apprentices.

a.bridge: BLS measures the employment and wages of the broader privately owned NAICS 611500 population, not 611513, and it does not measure AI task exposure. I mapped its occupation mix to task families: office/records, business operations, curriculum drafting, assessment, learner communications, and reporting have meaningful language-model exposure, while live trade instruction, coaching, and applied evaluation have less. Approximate occupation wages were used to give higher-paid management and professional work more weight. The broader technical-school population may overstate classroom-content exposure and understate apprenticeship compliance work; the net error direction is uncertain.
caveats: The occupation source is NAICS 611500 rather than 611513 and includes cosmetology, flight, and other technical schools.
The estimate concerns current not-yet-automated tasks; it does not assume that all generative-AI-assisted work becomes labor substitution.
The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization divisor, so it has the stated vintage and basis limitations.
a.change_evidence: A 611513 employer payroll extract joined to O*NET task statements, with employee hours and wages tagged by workflow and current automation status, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: In private technical and trade schools, educational occupations were 43.94% of employment, office and administrative support 14.34%, business and financial operations 10.49%, and management 10.60% in May 2023. The base reflects high exposure in records, scheduling, communications, reporting, curriculum first drafts, and formative assessment; moderate exposure in instructor preparation and program design; and low exposure in hands-on demonstration, safety supervision, mentoring, and final competency judgment.

d5.bridge: National registered-apprentice counts and federal expansion goals measure participants in a broader system, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current outsourced service basket of LMM 611513 firms. I translate strong participation growth and policy support into a moderate five-year demand increase, haircutting for employer-captive delivery, public providers, funding cyclicality, and the distinction between people served and purchased external training. The federal goal could overstate realized demand, while the historical near-doubling may understate expansion into new occupations.
caveats: Active apprentices, total people served, programs, and outsourced training demand are different quantities.
Policy goals are not forecasts and may change with appropriations or administration priorities.
The estimate holds service quality and price constant and does not count new AI-training content as price inflation.
d5.change_evidence: RAPIDS time series separating active apprentices, related-instruction provider type, external provider spend, constant-price tuition, program starts and closures, plus signed employer training contracts through 2031 would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Apprenticeship.gov reported 660,500 active apprentices in FY2024, nearly double a decade earlier. GAO reported about 940,000 people enrolled or served in registered programs in FY2024, and DOL's FY2026 budget narrative stated a goal to reach and surpass one million active apprentices. These signals support growth, but not one-for-one growth in outsourced provider revenue, so the base is 1.20 rather than extrapolating the headline goal.

caveats: No source measures lens eligibility among $1-10M EBITDA firms in NAICS 611513.
SUSB private-firm counts can include organizational forms whose economics or governance are not practically transferable.
The frozen firm count is a margin-bridged estimate using broader size-class data and an education-sector margin.
e.change_evidence: A record-level reconciliation of the 193 estimated LMM-band firms against RAPIDS sponsor type, IRS tax status, ownership, customer concentration, external revenue, and change-of-control assignability would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is service-oriented, but the apprenticeship ecosystem includes single employers, employer consortia, unions, community colleges, community organizations, workforce boards, and government agencies. The lens retains only privately controlled, externally serving, recurring providers with transferable operations; it excludes captive programs and institution-bound or grant-only entities. A substantial but minority-to-slim-majority share of the frozen LMM firm estimate is therefore likely eligible.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 611513 ($1,012,260,132) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 611513 ($2,337,605,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1657 (margins.json[61]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Education EBITDA/Sales 16.57%); revenue/employee $161,103 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 611513); class <5: 677 firms, avg revenue/firm $364,332 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $60,370 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 278 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,055,547 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $174,904 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 187 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,830,444 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $303,305 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 167 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,230,198 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,032,344 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $20,886,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,460,854 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,225,455 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,528,658 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 193

caveats: No observed source measures the share of apprenticeship-training quantity that will still require an external accountable operator in five years.
Regulatory requirements establish accountability but do not guarantee that a lens firm, rather than an employer, union, or college, supplies it.
Software may complement human mentors more than it replaces them, while administrative intermediation may be more substitutable.
o.change_evidence: Employer procurement surveys and RAPIDS records that track shifts from external providers to employer self-administration, AI-only course delivery, mentor hours, audit findings, and competency outcomes would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Apprenticeship training combines applied training and course work, and registered programs require structured on-the-job learning or mentorship, classroom instruction, progressive wages, and accountable sponsorship. Software can self-serve portions of content, practice, scheduling, records, and support, and employers can administer programs internally, but most year-five quantity should still require an accountable training or coordinating operator. The low case allows substantial employer insourcing and software-led instruction without assuming that hands-on verification disappears.

caveats: The sponsor payment survey is old and covers registered apprenticeship broadly, not LMM NAICS 611513 providers.
Public funding rules vary substantially by state and program, and some savings may be passed through as lower reimbursable cost.
The estimate excludes demand loss and implementation difficulty, which belong in other primitives.
q.change_evidence: A contract-level sample showing pricing basis, renewal cadence, cost-disclosure clauses, grant allowability, reimbursement mechanics, customer concentration, and gross-margin changes following automation would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Employer-paid instruction and fixed per-apprentice or per-cohort arrangements can let an operator retain workflow savings during a contract term. Retention is diluted at renewals by competitive bidding, tuition support, grant cost rules, customer demands, and subsidized public alternatives. A sponsor survey found employers covered direct related-instruction costs in 72% of programs, while 23% cited apprentice payment, 11% joint funds, and 9% public funding; current state support ranges from tuition subsidies to reimbursements. Those structures support partial, not full, five-year retention of implemented gross benefit.

rho.bridge: Census measures current and near-term AI use across all U.S. employer businesses, while federal education and apprenticeship sources describe permitted or encouraged applications rather than realized labor implementation in 611513. I translate those adoption signals into the share of exposed apprenticeship-training opportunity implemented over five years. National adoption likely understates eventual use in document-heavy training administration, but broad policy enthusiasm likely overstates labor realization because quality, privacy, instructor acceptance, integration, and human accountability remain constraints.
caveats: The Census adoption rates cover all industries and changed question wording in November 2025 to include any business function.
Federal guidance and portals demonstrate enablement, not measured productivity or headcount avoidance.
Small LMM providers may adopt later than larger institutions because integrations and data governance have fixed costs.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal sample of 611513 firms showing AI workflow deployment, pre/post labor hours, error rates, completion outcomes, privacy incidents, and instructor override rates would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: AI use in any business function was 17%-20% across U.S. businesses from December 2025 to May 2026, with expected use at 20%-23%, showing adoption is real but incomplete. Education guidance explicitly identifies personalization, classroom efficiency, lower administrative burden, and teacher training, and DOL launched tools for integrating AI into registered apprenticeship in 2026. Over five years, about half of the exposed opportunity is implementable, with human review and hands-on delivery limiting the upper end.

s5.bridge: Census owner-age data cover U.S. employer businesses rather than 611513 LMM firms and measure age, not qualifying control transfers. I use the broad prevalence of owners age 55 or older as a succession-pressure indicator, then reduce it for five-year timing, multi-owner continuity, nonprofit/institutional operators, failed or non-control transitions, and buyer diligence around sponsor relationships. The age proxy probably overstates transfer probability for institution-affiliated providers but may understate pressure at long-held independent schools.
caveats: Owner age is an economy-wide and dated proxy, not observed deal flow for this industry.
A transfer may require customer, grant, credential, lease, or sponsor approvals and therefore may not qualify even when ownership changes.
Deaths, closures, internal reorganizations, and transfers without operating continuity are excluded.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-transaction and ownership-history data from BizBuySell, GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, and state school or apprenticeship approvals, matched to 611513 sellers over 2016-2026, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Census reported that over half of U.S. business owners were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey. That supports meaningful succession pressure, but only a fraction converts within five years into a qualifying sale of a transferable apprenticeship-training operation, yielding a base probability of 22%.

principal_driver: Growing apprenticeship participation combined with document-heavy administration and curriculum workflows creates repeatable AI-enabled labor leverage.
principal_weakness: The addressable ownership pool and commercial retention are obscured by a mixed ecosystem of captive, public, union, nonprofit, and grant-funded delivery models.
